rules_fired.because: Light-sensitive reagents degrade under UV or visible light by different mechanisms depending on the compound class. Fluorophores (DAPI, ethidium bromide) undergo irreversible photobleaching — the chromophore is destroyed; 30 seconds of UV transilluminator exposure causes measurable DAPI signal loss. Oxidizers (H₂O₂, NaOCl) photodecompose, losing titer. Peroxide-forming solvents (diethyl ether) form hydroperoxides faster in light. Light-activated monomers (acrylamide) can begin polymerising spontaneously. Amber containers or foil-wrap block the relevant wavelengths for all of these.
rules_fired.cite: Thermo Fisher Scientific: DAPI Technical Data Sheet (D1306). Lakowicz, Principles of Fluorescence Spectroscopy, 3rd ed., Ch. 1. Sigma-Aldrich H₂O₂ Product Information (216763).

rules_fired.because: Strong oxidizers (hydrogen peroxide 30%, sodium hypochlorite) react exothermically with organic materials and flammables, risking fire or explosion. H₂O₂ decomposes catalytically in contact with iron, copper, or manganese — use glass, polypropylene, or HDPE containers only. Bleach mixed with acids releases chlorine gas; mixed with ammonia or amines releases chloramines — never co-dispose in the same sink drain.
rules_fired.cite: Sigma-Aldrich Hydrogen Peroxide Safety Information (216763). OSHA Sodium Hypochlorite Hazard Bulletin. Bretherick's Handbook of Reactive Chemical Hazards, 7th ed.
